segments.text: Okay, so this is the book of the gradual sayings I'm reading from which is the Palitech Society translation
Of the Angutra Nikaya. This is the fifth book the book of the tens and eleven
So the first suita
That's have I heard on certain occasion the exalted one was staying near Savati a jeta growth in Anathapindika spa
Then the veritable Ananda came to see the exalted one and on coming to him saluted the exalted one
He sat down at one side
So seated the veritable Ananda said this to him
Praise her. What is the object? What is the profit of good conduct or moral conduct?
And the Buddha said why Ananda freedom from remorse is the object
Freedom from remorse is the profit of moral conduct
I stop here for a while to just
make a few
Commence the exalted one is a translation of the Palitim Bhagavad
Bhagavad is one of the ten names of the Buddha and
Here is mentioned that the Buddha was staying at
Savati in Jeta's growth or Jeta Vana
Anathapindika spa
This monastery is called Jeta Vana or Anathapindika spa because it was donated by Anathapindika
A very rich man who bought it from Prince Jeta. That's why it is also called Jeta Vana
And this monastery is the monastery where the Buddha spent most of the Vasa
45 years of Vasa
He spent most of them in Savati
So that's why you find many of the sutras mentioned Savati in Jeta Vana
Then remember Ananda asked the Buddha
About the object and profit of
Here it says good conduct
Actually it's moral
Another translation would be moral conduct and the Pali word is sila
Sila or moral conduct means
Extention from harming other living beings
Keeping the precepts which
Helps us not to harm other living beings
For example in the five precepts of a Buddhist layman
The first precept is not to kill
We abstain from killing
And the second precept is not to take what is not given to us
The third is not to commit adultery
The fourth is not to lie
The fifth is not to take intoxicants or drugs
That cause heedlessness
So these precepts help us to abstain from evil
From harming other living beings
So that is why when we abstain from harming other living beings
The profit of it is freedom from remorse
That is also you could say non-remorse
Not regretting
Now to continue
Then Verable Ananda asks
This translation here prays
Actually it's old English
Modern day English would be please
So it's a pray or please
What is the object?
What is the profit of freedom from remorse?
And the Buddha said joy Ananda
Joy is the object
Joy is the profit of freedom from remorse
Again he asks
But sir what is the object?
What is the profit of joy?
Delight Ananda is the object
Delight is the profit of joy
But pray sir what is the object?
What is the profit of delight?
Calm Ananda is the object
Calm is the profit of delight
But sir what is the object?
What is the profit of calm?
Happiness Ananda is the object. Happiness is the profit of calm.
Praise Sir, what is the object? What is the profit of happiness?
Concentration Ananda, Samadhi is the object. Concentration is the profit of happiness.
But praise Sir, what is the object? What is the profit of concentration?
Knowing and seeing things as they really are Ananda is the object and profit of concentration.
What is the object Sir? What is the profit of knowing and seeing things as they really are?
Revolving or awareness and dispassion or disenchantment Ananda is the object of it.
Praise Sir, what is the object and profit of revulsion and dispassion?
Release or liberation by knowing and seeing Ananda is the object and profit of these.
So you see Ananda. Moral conduct has freedom from remorse as object and profit.
Freedom from remorse has joy. Joy has delight. Delight has calm. Calm has happiness.
Happiness has concentration. Concentration has seeing things as they really are.
Seeing things as they really are has revulsion and dispassion.
Revulsion and dispassion have released by knowing and seeing as the object and profit.
So you see Ananda. Moral conduct leads gradually up to the summit.
That's the end of the sutta.
So to recapitulate, the sila or moral conduct leads to non-remorse.
And non-remorse leads to joy. The pali is kamoja, joy.
And joy leads to delight, piti.
I use the translation delight, whereas the book says rapture,
because delight is supposed to be a subtle, subtle feeling.
Whereas the later books say that piti could be very strong.
But in the Diga Nikaya, it is stated that this piti and sukha are subtle feelings that arises in meditation.
And then delight, piti, leads to kam, pasadi.
And kam leads to happiness, and happiness leads to concentration.
So you see basically sila gives us non-remorse, and that leads to delight.
And delight leads to kam, and which later leads to concentration.
Concentration samadhi is defined in the suttas generally as the four janas, or one pointiness of mind,
which is also explained as the four janas.
And then concentration is the condition for knowing and seeing things as they really are,
and which later leads to liberation or release.
So this samadhi is a very important factor for you to know and see things as they really are.
And that is why jana is a very important factor in the Noble Eightfold Path.
It is the last factor that enables us to know and see things as they really are before liberation.
And knowing and seeing things as they really are leads to nibbida.
Nibbida here is translated as revulsion.
You could also say awareness.
When you see things as they really are, that means you see that everything in the world has no stability.
Everything in the world is changing, it arises and passes away, arises and passes away.
So if a person sees that nothing you can hold on to in this world, everything must pass away.
So you get tired of it, you get weary of it, and that leads to viraga.
Raga is generally translated as passion or craving.
So viraga is dispassion, dispassion or disenchantment.
And when a person is disenchanted with the world, he starts, he lets go of the world.
And only when you let go of the world can you attain liberation by knowing and seeing.
So these conditions that finally lead to liberation is a kind of dependent origination.
Normally when we talk about dependent origination, we talk about paticha samupada,
which is the twelve links that lead you to the end of suffering.
Or he also explains how suffering arises.
But here it is a series of steps that lead you to liberation.
So sometimes this series of steps in this uttah is called transcendental dependent origination.
And something like this uttah is also found elsewhere in the Angutra Nikhaya 8.81 and 11.1,
and also Sanghuta Nikhaya 12.23.
So just to say again, this series of transcendental dependent origination starts with Sela.
So Sela is a very important factor that finally leads to liberation.
Some people, not being aware of this type of sutras, they think just by meditation alone
and not being bothered with other things like keeping Sela, doing dhana and all that,
they think they can get liberation.
You can see from this sutra that you need to keep good Sela to attain that happiness
that leads to concentration, and that concentration leads to seeing things clearly
so that you can be liberated.
The other thing I'd like to mention here is that you see by keeping Sela, joy can arise.
So even if a person leads the holy life, where's the rope?
And even if he does not succeed in meditation, does not attain dhana and all that,
just by keeping good Sela, that person has joy within him.
That is why people can continue to wear the rope because you have joy in wearing the rope.
If there is no joy in wearing the rope, nobody will want to wear the rope, yes or no?
So if we wear the rope, initially we have suffering because on the physical aspect,
you are disadvantaged in the sense that all your food is dependent on people's donation and all that.
And nothing you can buy on your own, you have to depend entirely on the support of others.
So for a person not used to it initially it could be a bit of a dukkha,
but by keeping good Sela, joy arises, then that person can continue to lead the holy life.
And then if he meditates and attains deep concentration, then greater joy arises.
In fact, the Buddha mentioned in some sutras like in the Majjhima Nipaya,
Buddha said the joy of attaining the dhana surpasses heavenly bliss,
by which he means the heavenly bliss of the sensual realm or the desire realm, like Sakadeva Raja.
He does not experience this jhanic bliss which is even higher than what Sakadeva Raja can experience.
So if a monk actually practices the holy life well, then he has great happiness within,
and that makes up for all the physical discomforts that he could experience.
Now we come to another sutra, 10.2.
The Buddha said,
For one who is virtuous in full possession of virtue, there is no need for the purposeful thought,
may freedom from remorse arise in me.
This month is in accordance with nature, that for one who is virtuous in full possession of virtue,
freedom from remorse arises.
Now this word, who is virtuous, you could also say for one who has moral conduct in full possession of moral conduct,
there is no need for the purposeful thought or intentional thought, may freedom from remorse arise in me.
This is in accordance with dhamma, here they translate as nature,
that for one who is virtuous, freedom from remorse arises.
So once you have virtue, you don't have to think that you want to have non-remorse.
Non-remorse automatically follows them if you have sila.
In the Buddha, continue.
Months, for one who is free from remorse, there is no need for the purposeful thought, may joy arise in me.
This month is in accordance with nature, that for one who is free from remorse, joy arises.
Months, for one who is joyous, there is no need for the purposeful thought, may delight arise in me.
This month is in accordance with nature, that for one who is joyous, delight arises.
Months, for one whose mind is delighted, there is no need for the purposeful thought,
may my body be calmed.
This month is in accordance with nature, that for one whose mind is delighted, the body is calmed.
Months, for one whose body is calmed, there is no need for the thought, may I feel happiness.
This month is in accordance with nature, that one whose body is calmed, feels happiness.
Months, for one who is happy, there is no need for the thought, may by mind be concentrated.
It follows that the happy man's mind is concentrated.
Months, for one who is concentrated, there is no need for the thought, may I know and see things as they really are.
It follows naturally that one who is concentrated does so.
Months, for one who knows and sees things as they really are, there is no need for the thought, may I feel revulsion, may passion fade in me.
It follows naturally that such a one feels revulsion and dispassion.
Months, for one who feels revulsion and dispassion, there is no need for the thought, may I realize release by knowing and seeing.
It follows naturally that he who feels revulsion and dispassion realizes release by knowing and seeing.
So you see monks, release by knowing and seeing is the object and prophet of revulsion and dispassion.
Revulsion and dispassion is the object and prophet of seeing and knowing things as they really are.
Seeing and knowing things as they really are is the object and prophet of concentration.
Concentration is the object and prophet of happiness.
Happiness is the object and prophet of calm.
Calm is the object and prophet of joy.
Joy is the object and prophet of freedom from remorse.
Freedom from remorse is the object and prophet of moral conduct.
One state just causes another state to swell.
One state just causes the fulfillment of another state for the sake of going from this side to the beyond.
That's the end of the sutta.
The beyond or the further shore refers to nipana.
So you see this sutta, it says that one condition arises and another follows naturally without having to make a wish for it.
And this is a bit similar to the sutta in the Angutra Nikaya 5.43 where the Buddha said,
if you want certain things in this world, for example, to have a long life, to be happy, to be wealthy, and after death to have a good rebirth, etc.
The Buddha said it's no use praying for it or making a vow.
You have to walk the path that brings you to what you want to get.
For example, if you want to have a long life, you must not kill.
If you kill, then even you pray and you make a vow, you will still get short life because you don't have the condition necessary to give you a long life.
So from this sutta, you know that you have to work for whatever you want to get.
And only if there is a condition for it, you will get what you want to get.
For example, if you want to be happy and to have a happy rebirth, then you must give happiness to other beings, then you deserve happiness.
Otherwise, there is no condition for happiness.
It's just like a person, if he sees the newspaper, there's a vacancy for a doctor.
But he does not have the qualification of a doctor.
How can he apply for the doctor's job?
That's enough.
However, the person is applying for a lawyer's job, he has to have the qualification of a lawyer before he can apply for a lawyer's job.
So in the same way, whatever we want, we got to have the necessary condition to get what we want.
So this sutta is telling us if you want to be happy, then we got to have silla.
We have moral conduct.
So because we don't harm others, then we have non-remorse and non-remorse, deeds to joy, etc.
And the rest of the sutta is a bit similar to the previous one.
So this is another sutta that also shows if you want to attain liberation, then you got to have all these conditions that leads to liberation.
Now we go to another sutta, it's 10.8.
The Buddha said,
Mums, a monk may be a believer, but yet not be virtuous, thus in that respect he is incomplete.
That defect must be remedied by the thought, how can I be both a believer and virtuous?
But monks, when a monk is both of these, he is complete in that respect.
Let's stop here for a moment.
Here to be a believer, a monk may be a believer.
Here actually you could say a monk may have faith and yet not be virtuous and yet not have moral conduct.
So that monk has faith, but he has only one, the only thing he has.
So he has to develop moral conduct, then he is complete up to that respect.
Then the Buddha continued,
Mums, a monk may be both a believer and virtuous, yet not learned.
He may be a believer, a virtuous and learned, yet no Dharma preacher.
He may be a believer, a virtuous, learned and a Dharma preacher, yet not a frequenter of debates.
He may be all of these, yet not be confident in expounding Dharma in a company.
He may be all of these, yet not expert in monastic discipline, Vinaya.
He may be all of these, yet no forest dweller, a lodger in solitude.
He may be all of these, yet not be one who attains at will, without difficulty and without trouble,
the four jhanas which belong to the higher mind, which even in this same visible state are blissful to abide in.
Nor does he, by destroying the asavas in the same visible state,
comprehending it of himself, realize the release by mind, the release by wisdom, and attaining it abide therein.
So in that respect, he is incomplete.
That defect must be remedied by the thought, how can I be both a believer and virtuous,
also learned, a Dharma preacher, a frequenter of debates, and confident in expounding Dharma in a company.
Also expert in the monastic discipline, a forest dweller, a lodger in solitude,
and one who attains at will the four jhanas, and by destroying the asavas,
realize the release by mind, the release by wisdom, and attaining it abide therein.
Then monks, when he has attained each one of these ten things, he is complete in that respect.
Monks, if he be endowed with these ten qualities, a monk is all together charming and complete in every attribute.
That's the end of the sutra.
So you find this sutra is giving practical advice to monks to attain these various qualities that make for veneration.
And some of these qualities are also repeated elsewhere.
So these qualities are very good qualities that a monk should develop.
The first one, he should have faith.
Having faith means having faith in the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, the triple jhana.
Having faith in the Buddha means having faith or believing that the Buddha is enlightened.
And then having faith in the Dharma means believing in the things that are taught in the Dharma,
having faith that they are the words of an enlightened person.
And then having faith in the Sangha is having faith in the Bhikkhu Sangha.
Sometimes people think that the Sangha only refers to Arya.
But actually you find in like the Majima Nikaaya, I think sutra number 142, Dakkina Vibangasutha,
where the Buddha says that the Sangha refers to the community of monks or nuns.
So because if you take refuge with the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha,
if you just take refuge with the Arya Sangha, who is the Arya?
You don't know who is the Arya.
Whereas if you say you take refuge with the Sangha of monks,
then you know those people who wear the robe.
That's what you find in the sutras, for example, some Anagami, lay disciples like Chitta or Ataka,
when monks come to the house or arms round and they pay respect to the monk.
They don't think, oh, I'm an Anagami.
This monk is only a Putujana or a Sutta Panna,
not worthy of my being respected and everything like that.
Because the monk has renounced and he represents the Anansian disciples of the Buddha,
so they pay respect to the monk.
So that is having faith.
And then the second one, having moral conduct.
Having moral conduct is keeping the precepts and basically the precepts that are important
are those precepts which help us not to harm other living beings.
And then number three is learning.
Or sometimes they say much learning.
The Pali word is bahu sutta.
Or sometimes the equivalent is bahu satcha.
Bahu means much.
Sutta means hearing, much hearing of the words of the Buddha.
And bahu satcha is much hearing of the, satcha is truth.
Truth means the truth found in the Dhamma or the Buddha's words.
So it also means the same thing.
Being knowledgeable about the Buddha's teachings, the Buddha's words in particular.
Later we will find some other sutta where some of these terms are explained in more detail.
The Dhamma preacher.
So after a monk has faith, then he keeps a sealant.
Then after he has sealant, he should be learned in the Buddha's teachings.
And after he's learned, then he should try to teach the Buddha's teachings to others.
The Dhamma preacher.
And after that, he should be a frequenter of debates.
Actually here a frequenter of debates, he could also say that he is one who discusses the Dhamma with others.
So because discussion of Dhamma is also very important to help us understand the Dhamma.
Sometimes there are certain points of Dhamma.
When we read the suttas, we are not very clear.
So when we discuss with other people who also study the suttas,
then sometimes they might understand certain topics a bit better than us
and we could learn something from them.
As they say, more heads are better than one.
So here the number five is frequenter of debates or frequenter of Dhamma discussions.
And then after that, confident in expounding Dhamma in a company.
This one also needs some training.
Usually mums are not used to teaching Dhamma to a crowd of people.
And initially you will have a lot of fear when you face a crowd of people.
So it takes practice slowly to get used to teaching to many people.
That's number six.
Then number seven is expert in vinaya.
Vinaya is monastic discipline.
That means for the discipline that is taught to monastics, those who are mums and nuns.
And because this vinaya is important for a monk to understand
so that he knows how to conduct himself,
otherwise if a person is not knowledgeable about vinaya,
he conducts himself like a layperson.
And people, by noticing his conduct or his deportment,
people might be sort of put off.
So it is important for a monk or a nun to know how to conduct himself.
That is taught in the vinaya.
And then after that, he is first to be a forest-dweller, a lodger in solitude.
This is for those who want to attain liberation, to leave samsara.
Then it is good if they could stay alone.
But then also needs training.
If you don't have a certain degree of samadhi, of concentration,
then it's difficult to stay alone alone.
Then after that, he's encouraged to attain the four jhanas.
So if he stays in solitude, the chances of attaining the jhanas are much higher.
Then after that, that leads to release by mind and release by wisdom.
That's attaining of arahan wood.
So these are the qualities that a mom is encouraged to develop.
That's why a lot of the things in the sutras are very practical instructions.
As we study the sutras, we know how to practice the holy life.
